patches.find: WIN-058 is listed on the dome's wins page with a one-line claim but no supporting equations, derivation, measurements, or physical mechanism — it is effectively undefined.
patches.replace: WIN-058 is listed on the dome's wins page with a one-line claim but no supporting equations, derivation, measurements, or physical mechanism — it is effectively undefined. Unlike WIN-057 (which at least describes the two-zone topology), WIN-058 provides no justifying content: no formula for the 0.9941 scale factor, no geometric derivation from disc radius or firmament height, and no specification of what physical quantity the 'unified angular coordinate' represents.
patches.rationale: ISS-378 (critical): The current text notes WIN-058 is undefined but doesn't contrast it with WIN-057 or specify what's missing. This strengthens the argument by enumerating exactly what a proper definition would require.

patches.find: The unified angular coordinate with scale factor 0.9941 was not derived a priori from dome geometry — it was fitted post-hoc to match WGS84 geodetic distances on six known routes. Any free parameter fitted to known data will reduce residuals; the question is whether 0.9941 was predicted before measuring. The dome site provides no derivation from disc radius, firmament height, or aetheric properties — only the fitted result. Furthermore, the V13 coordinate system still requires WGS84 distances as input (see the model's own OPEN-001 and OPEN-003 acknowledgments), making it dependent on globe geodesy rather than independent of it.
patches.replace: The unified angular coordinate with scale factor 0.9941 was not derived a priori from dome geometry — it was fitted post-hoc to match WGS84 geodetic distances on six known routes. Any free parameter fitted to known data will reduce residuals; the question is whether 0.9941 was predicted before measuring. The dome site provides no derivation from disc radius, firmament height, or aetheric properties — only the fitted result. The distinction matters: WGS84's flattening parameter (1/298.257) is derived from satellite orbit perturbations and independently confirmed by GPS, VLBI, and SLR to sub-millimeter precision — it is a measured physical quantity, not a convention. The dome's 0.9941 has no independent confirmation and no derivation from dome physics. Furthermore, the V13 coordinate system still requires WGS84 distances as input (see the model's own OPEN-001 and OPEN-003 acknowledgments), making it dependent on globe geodesy rather than independent of it.
patches.rationale: ISS-379 (major): The 'coordinate choice is convention' argument proves too much — WGS84 is also a 'convention' but makes testable predictions. This patch adds the critical distinction: WGS84's parameters are independently measured physical quantities; the dome's 0.9941 is not.

patches.find: Standard geodesy achieves sub-0.01% distance accuracy worldwide (Torge & Müller, 2012); the dome's 6.2% RMSE is 600× worse even after fitting.
patches.replace: Standard geodesy achieves sub-0.01% distance accuracy worldwide using the WGS84 ellipsoid with independently measured parameters (Torge & Müller, 2012); the dome's 6.2% RMSE is 600× worse even after fitting a free parameter to known routes.
patches.rationale: ISS-380 (major): Torge & Müller citation is accurate but lacked context about WGS84 parameters being independently measured. This reframes the comparison more precisely.

patches.find: Why 0.9941 specifically? The dome provides no derivation from disc radius, firmament height, or aetheric wave speed — only the fitted result. Any value between ~0.98 and ~1.02 would reduce residuals on WGS84-derived routes; the specific value is determined by the input data, not by dome physics.
patches.replace: Why 0.9941 specifically? The dome provides no derivation from disc radius (20,015 km), firmament height (9,086 km), aetheric wave speed, or any other dome parameter — only the fitted result. The value minimizes residuals on six WGS84-derived routes, making it a least-squares output, not a theoretical prediction. For comparison, the globe's flattening f = 1/298.257 follows from Earth's rotation rate and mass distribution and is confirmed by independent methods (satellite orbits, gravimetry, VLBI). No analogous derivation or independent test exists for 0.9941.
patches.rationale: ISS-381 (major): Engages directly with the parameter value origin — explains it's a least-squares fit and contrasts with the globe's independently derived flattening.

patches.find: Cross-equatorial fit improved by adding free parameter (equatorial ring radius); still 600× worse than standard geodesy
patches.replace: Added free parameter (equatorial ring radius 14,105 km) to fit known WGS84 routes; cross-equatorial error fell from 25–78% to 6.2% RMSE but remains 600× worse than Vincenty geodesy (sub-0.01%)
patches.rationale: ISS-373 (major): Summary finding focused on NH regression rather than the WIN's actual claim (cross-equatorial improvement). This rebalances to acknowledge the dome's claimed achievement while noting it's still curve-fitting and 600× worse than standard methods.

patches.find: Version 13 introduced two-zone topology with a new free parameter (equatorial ring radius = 14,105 km) to fit cross-equatorial routes.
patches.replace: Version 13 introduced two-zone topology with a new free parameter (equatorial ring radius = 14,105 km) — a value fitted to minimize residuals on six known city-pair distances, not derived from dome geometry.
patches.rationale: ISS-374 (moderate): Makes explicit that the equatorial ring radius is a fitted parameter, not derived from dome physics.

patches.find: The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes the coordinate system still requires WGS84 as input.
patches.replace: The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes the coordinate system still requires WGS84 as input, and OPEN-015 acknowledges that near-equatorial cities below ~12° latitude 'fall beyond the range' of the V13 model — a systematic failure in the tropics that the 6.2% RMSE figure averages away.
patches.rationale: ISS-375 (moderate): Adds the OPEN-015 limitation which reveals the dome's own acknowledged failure zone that is hidden by the headline RMSE number.

patches.find: Compare Vincenty (1975) geodetic formulae achieving sub-0.01% error globally with zero fitted parameters — the dome's best result is still 600× worse.
patches.replace: Compare Vincenty (1975) geodetic inverse formulae achieving sub-millimeter accuracy (better than 0.001%) globally on the WGS84 ellipsoid — the dome's 6.2% RMSE is thousands of times worse, and the dome's model still cannot handle equatorial routes at all (OPEN-015).
patches.rationale: ISS-376 (moderate): Corrects understated Vincenty accuracy (was 'sub-0.5%' in some places, actually sub-millimeter) and ties back to OPEN-015 failure.

patches.find: The global dipole moment declines at ~5% per century (NCEI/WMM), translating to roughly 15–20 nT/yr in globally averaged surface intensity — significantly below the 28 nT/yr threshold, which is only met in the SAA core.
patches.replace: The global dipole moment has declined ~5% per century since 1840 (Thébault et al., 2015, IGRF-12). Translating this to surface intensity: 5% of ~30,000 nT equatorial field over 100 years gives ~15 nT/yr as a global average — well below the 28 nT/yr threshold, which is only met at SAA-core stations like Tsumeb. The dome's monitoring code (Domain 8) checks only Tsumeb, confirming the test was never truly global.
patches.rationale: ISS-323 (major): Replaces the imprecise '15–20 nT/yr' with an explicit derivation (5% of 30,000 nT / 100 yr = 15 nT/yr), adds the IGRF-12 citation, and tightens the link to single-station monitoring.

patches.find: Magnetic field decay exceeds 28 nT/year globally.
patches.replace: The global geomagnetic field decayed by at least 28 nT in 12 months (PROS-007).
patches.rationale: ISS-324 (moderate): Our detail_claim paraphrased incorrectly. The dome's actual PROS-007 wording is about total decay in 12 months, not a rate. This aligns our claim text with the dome's actual language.

patches.find: PROS-007 was registered 6 March 2026 and confirmed within days using WMM2025 data available since late 2024 — part of a batch of five predictions (WIN-035 through WIN-039) all registered on the same date, suggesting systematic data mining rather than prospective prediction.
patches.replace: PROS-007 was registered 6 March 2026 and confirmed within 6 days using WMM2025 data publicly available since December 2024 — a 6-day turnaround from registration to confirmation. It was part of a batch of five predictions (WIN-035 through WIN-039) all registered on the same date, suggesting systematic data mining from already-published sources rather than prospective prediction.
patches.rationale: ISS-326 (moderate): Adds the specific 6-day gap and clarifies WMM2025 publication date, making the data-mining argument more concrete.

patches.find: The SAA core experiences 20–35 nT/yr decay, but this is a regional feature driven by reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary, not the global dipole.
patches.replace: The SAA core experiences 20–35 nT/yr decay (NCEI/WMM), but this is a regional feature driven by reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary, not representative of the global dipole.
patches.rationale: ISS-327 (minor): Adds NCEI/WMM citation to the SAA decay rate claim, replacing the generic NOAA homepage link.

patches.find: The Antarctic gravity low is primarily attributed to an isostatically uncompensated mantle plume beneath the East Antarctic Plateau, with secondary contributions from post-glacial isostatic adjustment (Ebbing et al., 2021, JGR).
patches.replace: The Antarctic gravity low has multiple well-characterized standard-physics causes: (1) a sub-lithospheric mantle plume beneath the East Antarctic Plateau producing a broad negative anomaly (Ebbing et al., 2021, JGR); (2) incomplete post-glacial isostatic adjustment from the Last Glacial Maximum ice sheet, measured directly by GRACE satellite gravimetry (Shepherd et al., 2012, Science); and (3) crustal thickness variations mapped by seismic receiver functions. These mechanisms are independently constrained and explain both the magnitude and spatial pattern of the anomaly.
patches.rationale: ISS-409 (major): Expands the incomplete single-cause explanation to enumerate three independent standard-physics mechanisms with citations, matching the curmudgeon's recommendation.

patches.find: The dome predicts 90% gravity loss; reality shows 0.53% variation. The model's own physics is off by a factor of 170×.
patches.replace: The dome predicts ~90% gravity variation (factor of ~10 in g between pole and rim); reality shows 0.53% variation (factor of 1.005). The ratio of predicted to observed variation is 90%/0.53% ≈ 170× — the model's own height function is off by more than two orders of magnitude.
patches.rationale: ISS-410 (moderate): Makes the 170× factor derivation explicit and dimensionally clear, showing it's a ratio of fractional variations.

patches.find: If gravity is proportional to aetheric column height (the model's stated mechanism), then g_rim/g_pole = 837/8,537 = 0.098 — a 90% drop in gravity at the Antarctic rim.
patches.replace: The dome model states that gravity arises from the aetheric pressure gradient, which depends on the column height H(r). If gravity scales with H(r) — the model's own stated mechanism for gravitational effects — then g_rim/g_pole = 837/8,537 = 0.098, a 90% drop in gravity at the Antarctic rim. Even if the dome claims a nonlinear relationship, any monotonic function of H(r) still predicts dramatically stronger gravity at the pole (H=8,537 km) than at the rim (H=837 km), far exceeding the observed 0.53% variation.
patches.rationale: ISS-411 (moderate): Preempts the dome defender's escape hatch ('maybe gravity isn't directly proportional to H(r)') by noting that ANY monotonic function of H(r) still predicts a massive pole-rim gradient.

patches.find: WIN-037 (PROS-007) claims the global field decayed by ≥28 nT in the 12 months since March 2025. WIN-042 claims the ongoing decay rate is ≥28 nT/year. These are mathematically equivalent: if the field dropped ≥28 nT in any 12-month window, the rate was ≥28 nT/year over that window.
patches.replace: WIN-037 (PROS-007) claims the global field decayed by ≥28 nT in 12 months. WIN-042 claims the ongoing decay rate is ≥28 nT/year. These are mathematically equivalent: if the field dropped ≥28 nT in any 12-month window, the rate was ≥28 nT/year over that window. Both share the same fundamental problem: the 28 nT/yr threshold is met only at SAA-core stations (Tsumeb: ~30 nT/yr), not globally (~15 nT/yr from IGRF dipole moment decline). The dome's monitoring code checks only regional stations, confirming both WINs test the same regional measurement.
patches.rationale: ISS-333 (moderate): Expands the thin detail_evidence to explain WHY the duplication matters — same threshold, same regional data, same monitoring station.

patches.find: The ≥28 nT/yr threshold is met in SAA stations (regional decay of 20–35 nT/yr) but the global dipole moment is declining at ~5% per century, approximately 15–20 nT/yr in average surface intensity (Thébault et al., 2015, IGRF-12).
patches.replace: The ≥28 nT/yr threshold is met at SAA-core stations (regional decay of 20–35 nT/yr per NCEI/WMM) but the global dipole moment is declining at ~5% per century, approximately 15 nT/yr in globally averaged surface intensity (Thébault et al., 2015, IGRF-12) — well below the 28 nT/yr threshold.
patches.rationale: ISS-335 (moderate): Tightens the '15–20 nT/yr' to '15 nT/yr' for consistency with the WIN-037 patch, and adds explicit 'well below threshold' conclusion.

patches.find: Solar elevation angle from density function H(r) matches observations.
patches.replace: Solar elevation angle derived from dome height function H(r) matches observations.
patches.rationale: ISS-369 (moderate): H(r) is a height function, not a density function. The dome's own site (05-model.txt, 04-coordinates.txt) defines it as firmament height.

patches.find: The model page states this "reproduces the globe formula exactly." That's because it is the globe formula.
patches.replace: The dome's derivation page uses this formula without modification. That's because it is the globe formula.
patches.rationale: ISS-367 (moderate): Replaces unverifiable direct quote ('The model page states...') with a factual description of what the dome does. The dome's actual wording may differ from our quotation.

patches.find: The physical explanation comes from MHD geodynamo simulations (e.g., Aubert et al., 2013, Nature), which derive hemispheric asymmetry from first principles via core-mantle boundary heat flux variations and reversed-flux patches.
patches.replace: The physical explanation comes from MHD geodynamo simulations (e.g., Aubert et al., 2013, Nature), which reproduce hemispheric asymmetry via core-mantle boundary heat flux variations and reversed-flux patches. (Note: these simulations are data-constrained numerical models, not purely first-principles derivations — but they are derived from Maxwell's equations and Navier-Stokes, with boundary conditions from seismology.)
patches.rationale: ISS-389 (moderate): Corrects overstatement that CHAOS-7/geodynamo simulations are 'from first principles' — they are physics-based but data-constrained. This is more honest and harder for a defender to attack.

patches.find: The dome model states the asymmetry ratio "matches" without specifying: (a) what numerical ratio is claimed, (b) over what time period, (c) using what definition of "hemispheric" on a flat disc where one hemisphere is the disc center and the other is the rim, or (d) what the dome model actually predicts the ratio to be. Without a specific predicted value derived from dome geometry, any observed ratio automatically "matches."
patches.replace: The dome model states the asymmetry ratio "matches" without specifying: (a) what numerical ratio is claimed, (b) over what time period, (c) using what definition of "hemispheric" on a flat disc (where the geometric 'northern hemisphere' is a central disc and the 'southern hemisphere' is the surrounding annulus — structurally asymmetric by construction), or (d) what physical mechanism produces a specific ratio. Without a predicted numerical value derived before measurement, any observed ratio automatically "matches" — this is a textbook example of a non-falsifiable claim.
patches.rationale: ISS-390 (moderate): Adds the critical geometric point — on a flat disc, the two 'hemispheres' are structurally different (disc vs annulus), which means any asymmetry is built in by construction.

patches.find: Centuries of precision astrometry — from Tycho Brahe through Hipparcos and Gaia — confirm that true celestial pole altitude equals observer latitude to arcsecond precision using proper pole-star corrections.
patches.replace: Centuries of precision astrometry — from Tycho Brahe through Hipparcos and Gaia — confirm that true celestial pole altitude equals observer latitude to arcsecond precision using proper pole-star corrections. The dome's PRED-NEW-001, registered 2026-03-12, appeared after extensive site development work and uses Polaris observations published in standard star catalogs for decades — this is retrodiction of known data, not prospective prediction.
patches.rationale: ISS-402 (major): Adds the post-hoc timing argument — PRED-NEW-001 was registered after measurements were already well-known, making it retrodiction rather than prediction.

patches.find: The claimed +0.27° 'systematic excess' at 35.9°N is well within this 0.74° envelope and is not systematic at all — it depends on the time of observation during Polaris's diurnal circle.
patches.replace: The claimed +0.27° 'systematic excess' at 35.9°N is well within this 0.74° diurnal envelope. Combined with atmospheric refraction (~0.02–0.03° at typical Polaris elevations) and realistic measurement uncertainty (±0.2–0.5° for sextant/theodolite measurements without sub-arcsecond tracking), the total error budget is ±0.3–0.5° — the +0.27° figure falls squarely within expected noise, not a 'systematic' signal.
patches.rationale: ISS-403 (major): Adds explicit combined error budget showing +0.27° is within noise. The original text mentioned these factors separately but didn't combine them into a single error estimate.
